title: PSA Doubling Time Calculator - Prostate Cancer Monitoring
tagline: Calculate PSA doubling time from two serial measurements to assess prostate cancer progression rate and aggressiveness.
intro: Enter the initial and current PSA levels with the time interval between measurements to calculate the PSA doubling time.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive values for all fields.
error.psaNotRising: Current PSA must be higher than initial PSA for doubling time calculation.
error.timeZero: Time interval must be greater than zero.

about.heading: About the PSA doubling time calculator
about.body:
PSA (Prostate-Specific Antigen) is a glycoprotein produced by prostate epithelial cells. In the context of prostate cancer, serial PSA measurements can reveal the dynamic behaviour of the disease — whether cancer cells are growing slowly, rapidly, or stabilising. PSA doubling time (PSADT) quantifies the rate of PSA increase by calculating how many months it takes for the PSA level to double, providing a continuous, objective measure of cancer kinetics that a single PSA value cannot supply.

The mathematical foundation is straightforward. If PSA grows exponentially (as cancer cell populations tend to do in the early phases of disease), then the relationship between successive PSA values and time follows a logarithmic function. The standard formula is: PSADT = (ln 2 × time interval) ÷ ln(current PSA ÷ initial PSA). Here, ln 2 ≈ 0.693 is the natural logarithm of 2 (reflecting doubling), and the denominator is the natural log of the PSA ratio — a measure of how much the PSA has grown over the observed interval. A larger PSA ratio in the same time period produces a shorter doubling time, reflecting faster growth.

Clinically, PSADT is used in three main settings. First, after radical prostatectomy (surgical removal of the prostate), patients whose PSA rises above a detectable threshold (biochemical recurrence) need risk stratification to decide between observation, radiation therapy to the prostate bed, and systemic androgen deprivation therapy. A short PSADT (under 6 months) after prostatectomy is associated with a higher likelihood that the cancer has spread beyond the pelvis and therefore favours aggressive systemic treatment. Second, in men under active surveillance for low-risk localised prostate cancer, a shortening PSADT can signal disease reclassification from low-risk to intermediate or high-risk, prompting biopsy or treatment. Third, in metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer, PSADT measured on androgen deprivation therapy correlates with time to castration resistance and overall survival, informing the decision to add chemotherapy or androgen receptor pathway inhibitors upfront.

Interpretation categories used in clinical practice differ somewhat between institutions and clinical contexts, but the widely cited thresholds are: PSADT above 12 months indicates slow, indolent disease that is less likely to cause harm in the near term; 6–12 months is intermediate and warrants close monitoring; 3–6 months signals rapid progression with moderate urgency; and under 3 months represents very aggressive kinetics that nearly always warrants prompt treatment escalation.

Several practical considerations affect the reliability of PSADT. Measurements taken very close together in time tend to be less reliable because random biological variability and laboratory assay imprecision can disproportionately influence the calculation. Conversely, measurements taken more than two to three years apart may miss important inflection points in disease behaviour. Most guidelines recommend using at least two — ideally three — sequential PSA values obtained with the same laboratory method and at least three months apart before concluding that a trend is real and calculating doubling time. Acute events such as prostatitis, urinary tract infections, or prostate biopsy can transiently elevate PSA and should not be used as the starting measurement. The calculator uses the standard two-point logarithmic formula, which provides a useful estimate; for greater statistical robustness, linear regression on log-transformed serial PSA values can incorporate more data points.

PSA doubling time is a powerful but contextual tool. A PSADT of 8 months means something different for a 45-year-old man with many decades of expected life ahead than for an 82-year-old man with multiple serious comorbidities. Shared decision-making, incorporating patient values, overall health, life expectancy, and the full clinical picture, should always accompany any interpretation of PSA kinetics.

examples.heading: PSA doubling time examples
examples.intro: Scenarios showing how PSA values and time intervals translate into doubling times and risk categories.

items.note: PSA doubled in 12 months — borderline slow/moderate. Active surveillance with close monitoring is appropriate.
Very rapid progression — PSA quadrupled in 6 months. Prompt evaluation and treatment escalation are indicated.
Slow progression. Long doubling time suggests indolent disease; surveillance is likely appropriate.
Just above the 12-month slow threshold — generally considered low-risk, but continued monitoring is warranted.

howto.heading: How to use the PSA doubling time calculator
steps: Obtain two sequential PSA measurements from the same laboratory and assay method, ideally at least 3 months apart.
Enter the earlier (initial) PSA value in ng/mL into the first field.
Enter the more recent (current) PSA value in ng/mL into the second field. This must be higher than the initial value.
Enter the time interval between the two measurements in months.
Click Calculate. The PSA doubling time in months and the corresponding clinical risk category appear below the inputs.

items.q: What PSA doubling time is concerning?
items.a: A doubling time below 3 months is considered very rapid and is associated with aggressive disease, distant metastasis, and worse survival outcomes. Values between 3 and 6 months are rapid and warrant urgent clinical evaluation. Doubling times of 6–12 months are moderate, while values above 12 months are generally considered slow and associated with indolent disease and better prognosis.

items.q: How many PSA measurements do I need?
items.a: This calculator uses two PSA values (initial and current) to calculate doubling time. For greater statistical reliability, clinical guidelines generally recommend using three or more serial measurements and applying a log-linear regression approach that smooths out random variability. Two data points are sufficient for a directional estimate but may be influenced by a single anomalous value.

items.q: Can PSA doubling time be used after radical prostatectomy?
items.a: Yes — this is one of its most important clinical applications. After prostatectomy, any detectable PSA suggests residual or recurrent cancer. A short post-operative PSADT (under 6 months) is associated with distant metastasis rather than local recurrence, and typically guides oncologists toward systemic therapy in addition to salvage radiation. A long PSADT after surgery suggests local recurrence that may be treated with radiation alone.

items.q: What can cause a falsely short PSA doubling time?
items.a: Transient PSA elevations from prostatitis, urinary tract infections, recent prostate biopsy, vigorous exercise, or ejaculation within 48 hours can make doubling time appear shorter than it truly is. Using a very short time interval between measurements also amplifies the effect of random assay variability. Always use PSA values measured at rest, free from acute confounders, and preferably by the same laboratory.

items.q: Is PSA doubling time the same as PSA velocity?
items.a: No. PSA velocity is the absolute rate of PSA increase (ng/mL per year), while PSA doubling time reflects the relative rate of increase (how long it takes to double). PSA velocity is more useful at low PSA levels where small absolute changes are clinically significant, whereas PSA doubling time is better suited to tracking growth kinetics at higher PSA values. Both metrics provide complementary perspectives on disease dynamics.

items.q: Should PSA doubling time change my treatment decision?
items.a: PSA doubling time is one input into a complex treatment decision that also incorporates Gleason score, tumour stage, patient age, comorbidities, and personal preferences. It should never be used in isolation. For example, a short PSADT may prompt treatment escalation in a young, otherwise healthy man but may not change management in an elderly man with limited life expectancy. Discuss your PSA trend with a urologist or oncologist before drawing clinical conclusions.
